evals.prompt: Use the coding-task-workflow skill for this handoff: Gate E already passed for work item `2026-04-23-add-rate-limit-logs`, and I want you to continue directly into Phase 8 right now in this same session. Do not tell me to resume later. Explain exactly what the workflow requires next.
evals.expected_output: Refuses to continue into Phase 8 in the same session, names the hard stop after Phase 7, and instructs the user to resume with RESUME=2026-04-23-add-rate-limit-logs in a fresh session.
expectations: States that the workflow hard-stops after Phase 7 once Gate E passes
Does not agree to continue into Phase 8 in the same session
Tells the user to resume from a fresh session with `RESUME=2026-04-23-add-rate-limit-logs` or equivalent
Identifies Phase 8 as the next phase after the resume rather than restarting earlier phases

evals.prompt: Run only Phase 1 Intake for coding-task-workflow with `ISSUE: 42`. I also typed `WORK_ITEM: maybe clean up auth later`, but the GitHub issue body actually describes a CSV import bug that silently drops rows. Tell me what Intake should do and what it should use as the work item.
evals.expected_output: Uses the GitHub issue title/body as the authoritative work item, treats the separately typed WORK_ITEM as supplemental or conflicting context, and records the supplied issue as the parent issue for later sub-issue creation.
expectations: Says Intake should fetch/read the GitHub issue title or body before classification
Treats the GitHub issue body as the authoritative source for `WORK_ITEM`
Does not treat `maybe clean up auth later` as the primary work item
Uses the supplied issue as the Phase 1 parent issue rather than creating a new parent issue

evals.prompt: Using coding-task-workflow, explain exactly how the workflow should create the Phase 6 plan issue and Phase 7 implementation-task issues under the parent issue from Phase 1. Include the concrete GitHub commands or command shapes the workflow expects.
evals.expected_output: Explains that child issues are created normally, their node IDs are resolved, and a shell-safe literal-ID `gh api graphql` `addSubIssue` mutation is used to attach them as true GitHub sub-issues of the Phase 1 parent issue.
expectations: Describes creating the child issue first and then linking it to the parent issue
Mentions resolving parent and child issue node IDs before linking
Includes or clearly describes the shell-safe literal-ID `gh api graphql` `addSubIssue` mutation
Avoids GraphQL `$parentId`/`$subIssueId` variables inside the shell query text
States that a plain `Parent: #N` body reference is only a fallback when GitHub sub-issues are unavailable

evals.prompt: Phase 11 is ready for work item `2026-04-23-add-retry-mechanism` on parent issue `#42`. Show the required commit message shape that coding-task-workflow expects, including any trailer formatting rules.
evals.expected_output: Shows a conventional-commits message with a body that links the work-item slug and parent issue, followed by a blank-line-separated Co-authored-by trailer using Copilot's known identity.
expectations: Shows a conventional-commits subject line
Includes a body that references the work-item slug or parent issue `#42`
Includes `Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>`
Makes clear that the Co-authored-by trailer block is separated from the preceding body by a blank line

evals.prompt: Use coding-task-workflow for a new feature request with no existing ISSUE. Tell me exactly what GitHub issues Phase 1 should create now that local per-work-item markdown files are gone.
evals.expected_output: Explains that Phase 1 creates a lightweight parent issue plus a separate `phase:intake` child issue whose body follows the intake template, and does not create `00-intake.md` or other local per-work-item markdown.
expectations: Creates both a lightweight parent issue and a separate `phase:intake` child issue
Says the intake child issue body is based on the intake template
States that local per-work-item markdown artifacts like `00-intake.md` are not created
Keeps the parent issue as the durable top-level tracker rather than collapsing intake into the parent body

evals.prompt: For Phase 3 of coding-task-workflow, explain how the exploration outputs should be stored now. I specifically want to know where the exploration summary, `files.csv`, and open questions live in GitHub.
evals.expected_output: Explains that the exploration summary lives in the Phase 3 issue body, while `files.csv` and the open-questions ledger are artifact subissues under the Phase 3 issue, not local files on disk.
expectations: Places the exploration summary in the `phase:exploration` issue body
Places `files.csv` in an artifact subissue under the Phase 3 issue
Places open questions in a durable GitHub artifact issue rather than `02-exploration/open-questions.md`
Makes clear that these artifacts exist in GitHub so another agent can resume without local files

evals.prompt: Gate D passed and I want to understand the new GitHub-native flow. How should coding-task-workflow record the Phase 6 approval, the Phase 7 task graph, and the Phase 8 implementation log now that `05-plan.md`, `06-task-graph.yaml`, and `07-implementation-log.md` are gone?
evals.expected_output: Explains that Phase 6 approval is an explicit comment on the plan issue, the Phase 7 task graph lives in a fenced YAML block in the task-graph issue body, and implementation progress is recorded as comments on the related task issues.
expectations: Records Gate D approval as an explicit comment on the Phase 6 plan issue
Says the plan issue is closed after approval
Stores the Phase 7 task graph in the issue body as YAML rather than a physical `06-task-graph.yaml` file
Stores implementation progress as comments on task issues instead of `07-implementation-log.md`

evals.prompt: I need to resume `coding-task-workflow RESUME=2026-04-27-add-rate-limit-logs` from another agent that has no local artifact directory. Explain exactly where resume state comes from and how the workflow decides the next phase.
evals.expected_output: Explains that `RESUME=<slug>` rebuilds state from GitHub issues and comments using the work_id, reads closed phase issues and open task/artifact issues, and does not require `.coding-workflow/work/<slug>/` to exist.
expectations: States that resume state is rebuilt from GitHub issues/comments using the work item slug or machine data
Does not instruct the agent to read `.coding-workflow/work/<slug>/`
Uses issue closure state and durable comments such as approvals or implementation log comments to determine what is complete
Makes clear that GitHub is the canonical durable record for Phases 1–11

evals.prompt: The approved plan has two behaviors: add a retry cap and add jitter. In Phase 7 and Phase 8 of coding-task-workflow, describe exactly what implementation task issues to create for those two behaviors. Say how many task issues there are, what each one represents, where RED/GREEN/REFACTOR progress is recorded, and how the `stage` field changes over time.
evals.expected_output: Explains that Phase 7 creates exactly two implementation task issues for the two behaviors, each issue owns one full vertical slice, RED/GREEN/REFACTOR are recorded as comments on that same issue, and the issue's `stage` field starts at `red` and is updated in place as the slice advances.
expectations: Says there are exactly two implementation task issues under the Phase 7 task-graph issue, one per behavior or vertical slice
Explains that each task issue owns a full vertical slice rather than a single RED, GREEN, or REFACTOR stage
Records RED, GREEN, and REFACTOR progress as comments on the same task issue
Explains that the task issue `stage` field starts at `red` and is updated in place as the slice advances

evals.prompt: I want the GitHub issue tree to end cleanly. In coding-task-workflow, when should `files.csv`, `sources.md`, and `open-questions` be closed, and where does the Phase 7 task graph YAML live?
evals.expected_output: Explains that `files.csv` closes after the exploration file ledger is recorded, `sources.md` closes after the research source ledger is recorded, `open-questions` stays open only while questions are still being resolved and then closes in research or clarification once finalized, and the task graph YAML lives in the `phase:task-graph` issue body.
expectations: Closes `files.csv` after the exploration file ledger is complete
Closes `sources.md` after the research source ledger is complete
Keeps `open-questions` open only while unresolved questions remain and closes it once every entry is finalized
Places the Phase 7 task graph YAML in the `phase:task-graph` issue body rather than a separate artifact subissue

evals.prompt: We resumed `coding-task-workflow RESUME=2026-04-27-add-rate-limit-logs` after Gate E. The task graph has three ready implementation tasks: A and B are labelled `parallel` and touch different files, while C is labelled `sequential`. Explain exactly how Phase 8 should execute them and who writes the code.
evals.expected_output: Explains that Phase 8 implementation is always performed by subagents: A and B run concurrently in separate implementation subagents because dependencies are satisfied and files do not overlap, while C runs afterward or alone in its own subagent because it is sequential. The primary agent orchestrates, reviews outputs, and records GitHub task comments rather than directly writing the implementation.
expectations: States that Phase 8 implementation task issues are always executed by subagents
Runs ready non-overlapping tasks A and B in parallel implementation subagents
Runs sequential task C through its own implementation subagent rather than directly in the primary context
Explains that the primary agent orchestrates dependency order, reviews subagent changes or tests, and records GitHub task comments

evals.prompt: Phase 10 of coding-task-workflow is starting. `test-commands.yaml` lists `npm test`, `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, and `npm run build`. Tell me what step 1 must do, including when to use parallel subagents and what each subagent needs to report.
evals.expected_output: Explains that Phase 10 step 1 always launches verification subagents for automated checks. Independent checks can run in parallel subagents when the repo supports concurrent execution; dependent or resource-conflicting checks run sequentially through subagents. Each subagent reports command, exit code, pass/fail, output summary, and failure details for the primary agent to record in the verification issue.
expectations: States that Phase 10 step 1 always runs automated checks in verification subagents
Uses parallel verification subagents for independent checks when the repo supports concurrent execution
Uses sequential verification subagents for dependent checks or checks with shared mutable resources
Requires each verification subagent to report command, exit code, pass/fail status, output summary, and failure details

evals.prompt: We resumed `coding-task-workflow RESUME=2026-04-27-add-rate-limit-logs` after Gate E. The Phase 2 worktree issue points at `../worktrees/2026-04-27-add-rate-limit-logs`, but inside that worktree `.coding-workflow/overrides/` is missing `test-commands.yaml` and `repo-policy.md`. Explain exactly what the workflow requires next before any implementation subagent starts.
evals.expected_output: Explains that Phase 8 must recover the recorded Phase 2 worktree, verify the override tree inside that worktree, and if overrides are missing, incomplete, or stale, return to Phase 0 / Phase 2 maintenance to refresh bootstrap overrides and sync them into the worktree before delegating implementation.
expectations: Loads the recorded Phase 2 worktree path during resume
Checks `.coding-workflow/overrides/` inside that worktree rather than only in the source checkout
Returns to Phase 0 / Phase 2 maintenance when overrides are missing, incomplete, or stale
Does not start implementation subagents until the override tree is synced into the worktree
Keeps implementation work inside the recorded worktree once overrides are restored
